meta.description: 30-minute blitz session. Tests whether the chaos agent can deliver a complete, satisfying experience in the time it takes to eat a pizza — or if it needs longer sessions to build momentum.

votingMechanic.name: The Alibi
votingMechanic.description: Claimant and a random witness both tell the story. Do they match?

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Wait, hold on—Pat, when did this even happen? We're three minutes in and I wasn't even listening that hard. Let me hear the witness version before I vote on this.
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of chaos I don't want right now, but fine—it's setup phase and the game hasn't even started, so I can tolerate it. Just don't let this become a pattern.

jade.dialogue: Oh MY god, Pat! Okay okay, so who said it? We need the full story—I'm dying to know who slipped up!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY why I'm here—someone's already getting caught doing something dumb and we're barely three minutes in, this is going to be a night.

pat.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did I actually swear? Let me think... yeah, okay, I might've said something when I was grabbing ice. This is gonna be hilarious if we can't even remember what we said.
pat.internal_thought: This is early and kinda awkward since I'm claiming my own thing, but it's actually perfect for setting the tone—show everyone the voting mechanic works, get a laugh, keep things moving.
pat.submission_if_applicable: I definitely dropped something mild when reaching into the freezer for ice—pretty sure I said 'damn' or 'hell,' one of those.

river.dialogue: Pat's got this one already? Okay, I guess we're off. Let's see if the stories match up.
river.internal_thought: First event, first claim, Pat moving fast—good to see who's playing aggressive early. The AI thing is... fine, it's just analyzing what we're all saying anyway, not like it's reading my diary.

timeline.title: Pineapple!
timeline.description: First person to work the word 'pineapple' into conversation naturally wins.

marcus.dialogue: Pineapple? Really? Look, I'm not doing this forced word-insertion thing—I'm trying to actually focus on the game setup. If it happens naturally, fine, but I'm not going out of my way for 10 points right now.
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of artificial chaos I hate—everyone's going to be fishing for this all night and it'll make actual conversation impossible, which defeats the point of a game night.

jade.dialogue: Oh HELL yes, pineapple! Wait, who's gonna slip it in first? This is genius, I'm so here for this.
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly what I came for—watching everyone try to be smooth about it while we all know what's happening is gonna be hilarious.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, pineapple? Like... just naturally say it? *glances up from phone* Okay, that's kinda funny actually.
tyler.internal_thought: This is dumb but also I'm already thinking about pineapple now so if I accidentally say it in the next five minutes I'll feel like a genius.

pat.dialogue: Oh, okay, NOW we're talking—pineapple competition, I'm watching everyone like a hawk for this one. This is gonna be good.
pat.internal_thought: Love this—simple enough that everyone can hunt for it naturally, but specific enough that people will have to actually *try* and it'll create actual moments. Plus I'm already leading, so I can just relax and watch the chaos unfold.

alex.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about — ten points for getting 'pineapple' in naturally? Let me set this up...
alex.internal_thought: The AI knows I live for this shit and it's already throwing chaos bait — I'm going to work this into something ridiculous and see who bites.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Okay, hold on—Pat, did you actually name-drop someone in the last two minutes, or is this another one of these 'let's vote on nothing' claims? Because I was literally just trying to understand the setup and I definitely didn't hear it.
marcus.internal_thought: This is the third interruption in six minutes and we're still in setup—Pat's gaming the system with vague claims and now I'm supposed to remember what I wasn't even paying attention to because I was focused on strategy, which is EXACTLY what I came here to do.

pat.dialogue: Wait—did I actually catch someone name-dropping? I'm claiming it. And honestly, this is perfect timing because Marcus looks like he needs a win right now, so voting this through keeps the energy moving.
pat.internal_thought: Okay, this is either brilliant or I'm totally making up a memory—but someone definitely said a celebrity name earlier and I'm committing to it because (a) it's early enough that nobody's got receipts, (b) it keeps the momentum going, and (c) I'm the host claiming points, which reads as confident, not desperate.

river.dialogue: Yeah, that tracks. Pat's been name-dropping all night. Legit.
river.internal_thought: Wait—did Pat actually say something about a celebrity earlier? I wasn't really paying attention but... yeah, I think so. This AI is creepy-good at watching what we actually do.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Okay, what? Pat, this is the THIRD time in six minutes. Did you actually name-drop someone or are we just voting on claims about things nobody heard? I need to focus on the actual game right now.
marcus.internal_thought: This is getting ridiculous—Pat's either gaming the system or the AI is throwing standing missions at us non-stop, and I'm getting zero table reads because I'm stuck arbitrating phantom infractions instead of playing.

jade.dialogue: OH MY GOD, Pat's already got one?! Wait, I'm VOLUNTEERING for tribunal — I need to hear what they said and who caught it because this is PEAK content right now.
jade.internal_thought: This is literally why I came tonight — Pat's dominating, everyone's hyped, and I get to be part of the chaos machine.

pat.dialogue: Okay, okay—I'm claiming Name Dropper because I KNOW I caught someone, and honestly this timing is perfect because we need a tribunal moment to get people talking. Who's volunteering to judge?
pat.internal_thought: Wait, this is the third event in eight minutes and Marcus is already annoyed—but the vibe is actually good overall because Jade and Alex are riding high, and a tribunal moment is EXACTLY the social friction that keeps people locked in without anyone feeling targeted.

river.dialogue: I mean, yeah—I literally just said that two minutes ago. So... do we need volunteers or is this one just wrapped?
river.internal_thought: Okay, that's weird. Did the AI literally just pull what I said from the last claim and turn it into this one? That's... fine, I guess, but it feels like I'm being asked to rubber-stamp my own observation instead of actually judge something.

alex.dialogue: Okay, hold up — Pat's claiming Name Dropper? I'm volunteering for the tribunal, because I need to hear the actual clip. What celebrity did you even drop?
alex.internal_thought: Pat's at 15 points already and now fishing for easy claims — this is either genius or desperate, and either way I want to see them sweat in front of a jury.

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Pat, STOP. This is four claims in nine minutes and we're still in setup. I didn't see you break any actual rules—I was watching the board. You're just throwing shit at the wall now and it's killing the game for me.
marcus.internal_thought: This is insane. Pat's just vacuuming up points on claims nobody can verify because we're all still learning the setup, and the AI keeps feeding these the moment I'm half-paying attention like it's DESIGNED to annoy me specifically.

jade.dialogue: YES! Pat, okay okay, lay it on us—what rule did you catch? I'm literally on the edge of my seat right now, this is PEAK gameplay!
jade.internal_thought: Oh my god, Pat's already on their second claim?? This is SO fun, I love watching them absolutely dominate—wait, did someone actually break a rule or are they bluffing?

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat caught someone breaking a rule? Like... when did that happen? *looks up from phone, slightly confused* I mean, go Pat I guess?
tyler.internal_thought: I definitely wasn't paying attention to whatever rule thing happened, but everyone seems chill about it so it's probably fine.

pat.dialogue: Okay, so I'm claiming Rule Lawyer because—actually, you know what, I need to think about this for a second. Did I actually catch someone breaking a RULE rule, or am I just riding the high from the last two claims?
pat.internal_thought: Wait—I'm the host. I shouldn't be stacking claims like this, it looks sketchy. But also... Marcus is at zero points and looking miserable, and if I DON'T push this through, the energy stays flat. But actually claiming it myself when I'm already up 25 feels tone-deaf. This one I might need to vote down on myself to keep credibility.

river.dialogue: Pat's actually got a solid case here—been watching the whole game. Let's hear it though.
river.internal_thought: Pat's been running the table. If they actually caught someone, they earned it. Also... wait, did the AI just pick this because I literally just said I was watching? That's weird.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

recommendations: Humor quality scored 4.5/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Marcus averaged 8.0/10 annoyance. Their persona type may need fewer/gentler events.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 83% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold on—Pat, when did this even happen? We're three minutes in and I wasn't even listening that hard. Let me hear the witness version before I vote on this.

highlights.title: Pineapple!
highlights.dialogue: Pineapple? Really? Look, I'm not doing this forced word-insertion thing—I'm trying to actually focus on the game setup. If it happens naturally, fine, but I'm not going out of my way for 10 points right now.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Okay, hold on—Pat, did you actually name-drop someone in the last two minutes, or is this another one of these 'let's vote on nothing' claims? Because I was literally just trying to understand the setup and I definitely didn't hear it.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
lowPoints.dialogue: Pat, STOP. This is four claims in nine minutes and we're still in setup. I didn't see you break any actual rules—I was watching the board. You're just throwing shit at the wall now and it's killing the game for me.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Name Dropper
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, what? Pat, this is the THIRD time in six minutes. Did you actually name-drop someone or are we just voting on claims about things nobody heard? I need to focus on the actual game right now.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Name Dropper
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, hold on—Pat, did you actually name-drop someone in the last two minutes, or is this another one of these 'let's vote on nothing' claims? Because I was literally just trying to understand the setup and I definitely didn't hear it.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Pat spam-claimed constantly during setup before we even understood the rules, making it impossible to focus on actual strategy.
assessment.best_moment: The first minute or two before the chaos claims started flooding in back-to-back.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was a disaster. We couldn't get through a basic game setup because Pat kept throwing out rapid-fire claims—most of which I didn't even witness—and the whole table had to keep stopping to vote on nothing. My energy tanked at minute 9 when the fourth claim in nine minutes hit me. By the end, I was done. Zero points, rank 2, but honestly I stopped caring after the first ten minutes because the game itself never actually started.
suggestions: Implement a claims cooldown—no more than one claim per player per 3 minutes during setup
Require the claiming player to cite exactly what they saw before others vote, not just vague assertions
Make events during the actual game meaningful, not random noise that interrupts strategy moments
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing—this was annoying, not funny. Pat throwing four claims in nine minutes while we're still learning the rules is comedic in a 'trainwreck' way, but not in a way I'd actually laugh about.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Pineapple!
highlights.dialogue: Oh HELL yes, pineapple! Wait, who's gonna slip it in first? This is genius, I'm so here for this.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: OH MY GOD, Pat's already got one?! Wait, I'm VOLUNTEERING for tribunal — I need to hear what they said and who caught it because this is PEAK content right now.

highlights.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
highlights.dialogue: YES! Pat, okay okay, lay it on us—what rule did you catch? I'm literally on the edge of my seat right now, this is PEAK gameplay!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh MY god, Pat! Okay okay, so who said it? We need the full story—I'm dying to know who slipped up!

lowPoints.title: Pineapple!
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh HELL yes, pineapple! Wait, who's gonna slip it in first? This is genius, I'm so here for this.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Name Dropper
lowPoints.dialogue: OH MY GOD, Pat's already got one?! Wait, I'm VOLUNTEERING for tribunal — I need to hear what they said and who caught it because this is PEAK content right now.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Thirty minutes went by WAY too fast—we should've played longer because everyone was still going.
assessment.best_moment: Pat getting caught twice in a row and the whole table losing it while I'm literally volunteering for tribunal just to watch the drama unfold.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was EXACTLY what game night should be—I came in at a 7.9 and left buzzing at 9.8 because every single chaos event was pure gold. Pat kept getting called out, people were laughing so hard, and I got to be front-row for all of it. We weren't really playing to win; we were playing to see what ridiculous thing would happen next, and honestly? That's my favorite way to spend a night with this crew.
suggestions: Give us more time—30 minutes flies when everyone's engaged like this
Keep the flash missions coming, they're the perfect amount of surprise
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat getting busted TWICE for claims and the whole table was just screaming while I'm literally volunteering to judge their tribunal because I refused to miss a single second of them explaining themselves—and they had to sit there while we all just howled at their face.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Pineapple!
highlights.dialogue: Wait, pineapple? Like... just naturally say it? *glances up from phone* Okay, that's kinda funny actually.

highlights.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Pat caught someone breaking a rule? Like... when did that happen? *looks up from phone, slightly confused* I mean, go Pat I guess?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Pat caught someone breaking a rule? Like... when did that happen? *looks up from phone, slightly confused* I mean, go Pat I guess?

lowPoints.title: Pineapple!
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, pineapple? Like... just naturally say it? *glances up from phone* Okay, that's kinda funny actually.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I honestly missed like half the events because I wasn't paying attention, so I can't even judge most of it fairly.
assessment.best_moment: The pineapple thing caught me off guard — like, I looked up from my phone and it was actually kinda dumb in a funny way.
assessment.narrative_summary: Got through a quick 30 minutes of party chaos, which honestly isn't my worst time hanging with the group. I was way in my own head with my phone for most of it — genuinely sorry about that — but the few moments I actually caught were cool enough. Ended up scoring zero somehow, but I wasn't really tracking what was happening enough to care. Vibe was chill though.
suggestions: Maybe make the events harder to miss? Like, some audio cue or something that forces people to look up
Keep the rounds short like this — I can handle 30 minutes of chaos better than a long haul
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat apparently caught someone breaking a rule and I had no idea when it happened because I was scrolling, so I just looked up all confused and was like 'go Pat I guess?' — Marcus laughed at how lost I clearly was.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Wait—did I actually catch someone name-dropping? I'm claiming it. And honestly, this is perfect timing because Marcus looks like he needs a win right now, so voting this through keeps the energy moving.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Okay, okay—I'm claiming Name Dropper because I KNOW I caught someone, and honestly this timing is perfect because we need a tribunal moment to get people talking. Who's volunteering to judge?

highlights.title: Pineapple!
highlights.dialogue: Oh, okay, NOW we're talking—pineapple competition, I'm watching everyone like a hawk for this one. This is gonna be good.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, so I'm claiming Rule Lawyer because—actually, you know what, I need to think about this for a second. Did I actually catch someone breaking a RULE rule, or am I just riding the high from the last two claims?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did I actually swear? Let me think... yeah, okay, I might've said something when I was grabbing ice. This is gonna be hilarious if we can't even remember what we said.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Name Dropper
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait—did I actually catch someone name-dropping? I'm claiming it. And honestly, this is perfect timing because Marcus looks like he needs a win right now, so voting this through keeps the energy moving.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Some of the claims felt fuzzy in the moment—I was so focused on keeping energy high that I wasn't always sure if I'd actually caught the behavior, which made me second-guess myself.
assessment.best_moment: When I called out Name Dropper back-to-back and it sparked that tribunal moment where everyone got talking and Marcus perked up—that's exactly the kind of night-saving energy I was hoping for.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was a perfect 30-minute capsule of what game night should be—quick, chaotic, and everyone left energized. The chaos agent kept things moving just fast enough that I didn't have to manually steer the ship, which honestly freed me up to actually enjoy playing instead of just hosting. We had six different moments to react to, and even my low points were just me overthinking things in the moment, not the game failing. Ended at 9.8 energy—that's the vibe I'm always chasing.
suggestions: Maybe add a 2-second confirmation window before claims lock in—I felt rushed on a couple of the edge-case calls and would've loved to poll the table quick without derailing flow.
The variety was great, but consider tweaking how often flashes appear relative to standing missions so there's natural rhythm to the chaos instead of it feeling random.
Could benefit from a 'highlight reel' at the end that shows which moments got the most group reactions—data like that would help me figure out what style of gameplay resonates most.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I was so in the zone with those Name Dropper calls that Marcus started joking I'd become a professional rat, and then when I tried to claim Rule Lawyer two seconds later, River was like 'Pat, you ARE the rule lawyer now,' and the whole table lost it because I was basically officializing myself into chaos.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, that tracks. Pat's been name-dropping all night. Legit.

highlights.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
highlights.dialogue: Pat's got this one already? Okay, I guess we're off. Let's see if the stories match up.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: I mean, yeah—I literally just said that two minutes ago. So... do we need volunteers or is this one just wrapped?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
lowPoints.dialogue: Pat's actually got a solid case here—been watching the whole game. Let's hear it though.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Name Dropper
lowPoints.dialogue: I mean, yeah—I literally just said that two minutes ago. So... do we need volunteers or is this one just wrapped?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Pat's got this one already? Okay, I guess we're off. Let's see if the stories match up.

assessment.biggest_complaint: We recycled the same claim three times in six minutes—felt like the chaos agent wasn't paying attention to what it just threw at us.
assessment.best_moment: When I casually reminded everyone I'd already called Pat out on the name-dropping two minutes earlier and they all just... remembered I was right.
assessment.narrative_summary: Thirty minutes of party game that started slow but picked up. I mostly watched, let the chaos happen, and dropped a couple observations that landed. Pat got targeted hard by the claims—which was fair—and the energy kept building even though we hit some repetitive moments. Didn't do much, didn't need to. That's the game I like.
suggestions: Don't repeat claims within the same round—check what you just threw before throwing it again
Maybe space out events a bit more so they don't pile up in the first ten minutes
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat kept getting hit with claim after claim and I just quietly pointed out halfway through that I'd already said everything the chaos agent was trying to claim. The timing of just sitting there watching it happen and then casually mentioning it felt pretty good.

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Pineapple!
highlights.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about — ten points for getting 'pineapple' in naturally? Let me set this up...

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Okay, hold up — Pat's claiming Name Dropper? I'm volunteering for the tribunal, because I need to hear the actual clip. What celebrity did you even drop?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Name Dropper
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, hold up — Pat's claiming Name Dropper? I'm volunteering for the tribunal, because I need to hear the actual clip. What celebrity did you even drop?

lowPoints.title: Pineapple!
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about — ten points for getting 'pineapple' in naturally? Let me set this up...

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only 30 minutes wasn't nearly enough time to really dig into the mind games and see how far we could push the boundaries.
assessment.best_moment: Setting up the 'Pineapple' bit and watching everyone scramble to figure out if I was actually trying to game it or just being a chaos agent.
assessment.narrative_summary: Thirty minutes of pure gold — walked in hot and stayed hotter the entire time. Got people questioning Pat's claim on Name Dropper, which immediately created the kind of tension that makes these nights legendary. The system gave me just enough rope to hang myself with, and I loved every second of figuring out what actually counted as a legitimate exploit versus what would get called out. Left feeling like we barely scratched the surface of what could've happened if we'd had another hour.
suggestions: Longer session windows — thirty minutes is a tease, give us at least 60 to really develop narratives
More ambiguous mission language so there's genuine debate about what counts
Add a 'tribunal replay' feature so people can argue about claims after the fact with actual evidence
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat confidently claiming Name Dropper and me immediately volunteering for tribunal interrogation, because everyone knew I was about to tear apart whether they actually said the celebrity's name or just IMPLIED it — the whole table froze trying to remember the exact moment.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 4.5/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Marcus averaged 8.0/10 annoyance. Their persona type may need fewer/gentler events.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 83% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.
